summary: Al-asma wa-l-sifat is the Islamic theological topic of the names and attributes of God — the terms by which God names Himself (e.g. al-Rahman, al-Aziz) and the qualities they predicate (mercy, might, knowledge, power). The relation of these attributes to the divine essence was one of the most debated questions in classical kalam.
description:
In Islamic usage a distinction is often drawn between the names (asmāʾ) by which God is called or addressed and the attributes (ṣifāt) that describe the qualities those names imply. The theological problem is how to affirm real attributes of God — living, knowing, powerful — without compromising His absolute oneness (tawhid) by introducing plurality or resemblance to creatures into the divine.

The schools of kalam divided sharply on this. The Mu'tazila are generally reported as the first to take a definite position, tending to identify the attributes with the divine essence rather than treating them as distinct eternal realities, so as to avoid positing a multiplicity of eternal entities alongside God; on this reading God knows by His essence rather than by a distinct attribute of knowledge. The Ash'ari school, by contrast, affirmed the attributes as real and eternal, subsisting in the essence yet not identical to it — a position later summarized in the formula that the attributes are "neither He nor other than He." The Maturidi school likewise affirmed real attributes with its own formulations, and the Hanbali/Athari tendency emphasized affirming the attributes as given in scripture.

A further point of contention concerned the so-called revealed or "ambiguous" attributes (ṣifāt khabariyya) such as God's face, hand, or His establishment upon the throne. Approaches ranged from affirming them "without asking how" (bilā kayf) while denying any resemblance to creatures, to interpreting them figuratively (taʾwīl). This entry describes these positions without ruling on which is correct.
editorial_notes: The tripartite/quadripartite mapping of schools (Mu'tazila, Ash'ari, Maturidi, Athari) is a standard heuristic but compresses considerable internal variation; individual theologians within each school held nuanced positions. The characterization of the Mu'tazila as identifying attributes with the essence, and the Ash'ari 'neither He nor other than He' formula, are drawn from secondary encyclopedic summaries and are stated as the schools' reported positions, not as adjudicated fact. The bilā kayf vs. taʾwīl contrast over ṣifāt khabariyya is genuine but is summarized at a high level here; a dedicated entry could treat named theologians and texts. Recent scholarship cited via the consulted Wikipedia overview (e.g. Suleiman 2024, Janos 2024) was noted but not independently examined, hence medium confidence on the finer distinctions.

relations.note: The oneness of the divine names and attributes is a classical dimension of tawhid.
How attributes are affirmed turns on preserving God's incomparability.
Affirming attributes while denying likeness to creatures guards against tashbih.
Ash'ari doctrine of real attributes subsisting in the essence.

historical_period.note: The attribute debate crystallized among the kalam schools from roughly the 8th to the 11th centuries CE.

sources.citation: "Attributes of God in Islam." Wikipedia. Encyclopedic overview of the Mu'tazila, Ash'ari, and Maturidi positions on the divine attributes, citing recent scholarship (Suleiman 2024; Janos 2024).
Gimaret, D. "Ṣifa." In Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd ed. Leiden: Brill.
